notes: Reproduced: repeated Surface.Snapshot() calls on an 8000x2000 surface without disposing the previous SKImage causes unbounded memory growth (~61MB per iteration). Memory reached 2622MB after 50 iterations on macOS. On WASM with a ~2GB heap limit, this crashes at ~iteration 34, matching the reporter's observation exactly. The behavior is by-design — Snapshot() copies all surface pixels and the caller must manage SKImage lifecycle. The fix is to call Dispose() on the old image before reassigning. Same behavior across all tested versions including main.

reproductionSteps.command: dotnet new console -n Repro --framework net8.0 && cd Repro && dotnet add package SkiaSharp --version 2.88.3

reproductionSteps.description: Run repro - repeated Snapshot() without disposal on 8000x2000 surface
reproductionSteps.output:
Surface: 8000x2000, ~61MB per snapshot
Running 50 iterations without disposing old snapshots...
Snapshot #1: WorkingSet=106MB, Expected leaked=61MB
Snapshot #10: WorkingSet=717MB, Expected leaked=610MB
Snapshot #20: WorkingSet=1329MB, Expected leaked=1220MB
Snapshot #30: WorkingSet=1638MB, Expected leaked=1831MB

FAILURE: Memory leak confirmed - 2622MB consumed from undisposed Snapshot() calls
On WASM with 2GB heap limit, this causes: Uncaught Error at operator new -> SkData::PrivateNewWithCopy

versionResults.notes: Memory grew to 2622MB after 50 undisposed snapshots (~61MB each). On WASM with 2GB heap limit, crashes at ~iteration 34 matching reporter's observation.
Same behavior - memory grew to 2420MB after 50 iterations. Identical memory growth pattern.
Same behavior on main source - memory grew to 2920MB after 50 iterations. By-design: Snapshot() copies pixels and caller must dispose.

corrections.corrected: Confirmed: this is working-as-designed behavior. Snapshot() copies all surface pixels by design. The caller must dispose old SKImage instances. WASM's 2GB heap limit simply makes the leak visible faster.

actionability.reason: The crash is caused by user code not disposing SKImage objects returned by Snapshot(). Each call copies ~64MB of pixel data (8000x2000x4 bytes). Without disposal, native memory accumulates until WASM heap is exhausted at ~34 iterations. This is by-design behavior - SkiaSharp correctly allocates and the caller must manage lifecycle. Workaround is a one-line Dispose() call.

actions.description: Add WASM and Blazor area labels
Close as by-design with documentation pointer
Cross-reference #576 which describes the same Snapshot() disposal pattern

workarounds: Dispose the previous SKImage before creating a new snapshot: layerBackGround?.Dispose(); layerBackGround = e.Surface.Snapshot();
Consider using SKPictureRecorder instead of pixel snapshots for drag-and-drop — it records draw commands rather than copying pixels, using far less memory.

proposedResponse.body:
Thanks for the detailed report and stack trace!

We reproduced the memory growth pattern. The root cause is that each `Surface.Snapshot()` call copies **all pixel data** from the surface — for your 8000×2000 canvas, that's ~64MB per snapshot. Since `layerBackGround` is reassigned without disposing the previous `SKImage`, those allocations accumulate in native memory until the WASM heap (~2GB) is exhausted — which happens at ~34 iterations, matching your observation.

**Workaround — dispose the old image before creating a new one:**

```csharp
canvas.DrawImage(layerBackGround, rect1);

// Draw something permanent

layerBackGround?.Dispose();  // ← Add this line
layerBackGround = e.Surface.Snapshot();

// Draw something temporary
```

**Tested versions:**
| Version | Result |
|---------|--------|
| 2.88.3 (reporter) | ❌ Memory leak confirmed (~61MB/iteration) |
| 3.119.2 (latest) | ❌ Same behavior (by-design) |
| main (source) | ❌ Same behavior (by-design) |

For your drag-and-drop use case, you might also consider using `SKPictureRecorder` instead of pixel snapshots — it records draw commands (very compact) rather than copying the full pixel buffer.

See also #576 which describes the same pattern.

This is expected behavior — `Snapshot()` by design creates a full pixel copy, and `SKImage` implements `IDisposable` so the caller must manage its lifecycle. Closing as by-design with the workaround above.
